coredata.dc:title: Colourful organic afterglow materials with super-wide color gamut and scaled processability from cellulose
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, K.
Ji, X.
Zhang, J.
Mi, Q.
Wu, J.
Zhang, J.
coredata.dc:description: Multi-colour afterglow materials have attracted widespread attention recently, because they not only can eliminate the interference of scattered light and background fluorescence, but also store the richer information. In this work, the phosphorescence emission of cellulose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) was transferred to the rhodamine derivatives via an efficient radiative energy transfer process, because of the strong electrostatic attraction interactions between the positive charged CDs and the negative charged rhodamine derivatives. As a result, multi-color afterglow luminescence with tunable emission and wide color gamut was realized from blue-green to green, yellow, orange and red. With the contribution of the excellent processability of polymer materials, multi-color afterglow films, coatings, fibers and patterns were readily obtained, indicating a practical application in advanced anti-counterfeiting and multiple-mode information encryption. Such a facile, universal and efficient strategy to construct multi-colour afterglow materials inspires us to understand the luminescence process and design novel afterglow materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorescence
Afterglow
Energy transfer process
Cellulose
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Energy transfer process of multi-colour afterglow

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U2004211 and 52173292 ), the National Key Research and Development Project (No. 2020YFC1910303 ), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (No. 2018040 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of energy transfer between phosphorescence of CDs and rhodamine derivatives; (b) Absorption, fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs, and absorption and fluorescence spectra of RhB; (c) Modified Jablonski diagram of energy transfer between phosphorescence of CDs and rhodamine derivatives; (d) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs, and absorption spectra of rhodamine derivatives; (e) Afterglow emission spectra of CDs/PVA and Rhs/CDs/PVA; (f) CIE color coordinates of afterglow emission of CDs/PVA and Rhs/CDs/PVA.

(a) Photographs of CDs/PVA under 365 nm UV lamp and after UV off; (b) TEM image and size distribution of CDs; (c) Emission spectra of CDs solution under different excitation wavelengths; (d) High resolution XPS spectrum and fitting results of C 1s of CDs; (e) Modified Jablonski diagram of the principle of phosphorescence emission of CDs/PVA; (f) Three-dimensional phosphorescence plots of CDs/PVA at room temperature and under air atmosphere; (g) Time-resolved decay spectra of CDs/PVA at room temperature and under air atmosphere (λex = 270 nm, λem = 486 nm).

(a) Photographs of CDs/PVA and Rhs/CDs/PVA with different concentration of Rhs under 365 nm UV lamp and after UV off; (b–f) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the CDs, and absorption spectra of Rhs; (g–k) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs/PVA and Rhs/CDs/PVA; (l–p) Time-resolved decay spectra of CDs/PVA and Rhs/CDs/PVA at room temperature and under air atmosphere (λex = 270 nm, λem = 486 nm).

(a) Multi-colour Rhs/CDs/PVA coatings; (b) Multi-colour Rhs/CDs/PVA fibers; (c) Colorful flower by using Rhs/CDs/PVA as security ink; (d) Anti-counterfeiting words on the ordinary printing papers; (e) Microarray data storage pattern according to the binary codes; (f) Information encryption by using Chinese traditional telegraph code.

head.ce:title: Colourful organic afterglow materials with super-wide color gamut and scaled processability from cellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Engineering Plastics and CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multi-colour afterglow materials have attracted widespread attention recently, because they not only can eliminate the interference of scattered light and background fluorescence, but also store the richer information. In this work, the phosphorescence emission of cellulose-based nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) was transferred to the rhodamine derivatives via an efficient radiative energy transfer process, because of the strong electrostatic attraction interactions between the positive charged CDs and the negative charged rhodamine derivatives. As a result, multi-color afterglow luminescence with tunable emission and wide color gamut was realized from blue-green to green, yellow, orange and red. With the contribution of the excellent processability of polymer materials, multi-color afterglow films, coatings, fibers and patterns were readily obtained, indicating a practical application in advanced anti-counterfeiting and multiple-mode information encryption. Such a facile, universal and efficient strategy to construct multi-colour afterglow materials inspires us to understand the luminescence process and design novel afterglow materials.

Multi-colour afterglow luminescence with tunable emission and wide color gamut was realized via an efficient energy transfer.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow materials with long-lived luminescence can avoid the interference from the excitation light and background fluorescence, indicating a huge potential in bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, information encryption, advanced anti-counterfeiting and other fields [1–4]. Organic afterglow emission includes two types, one is phosphorescence emission, in which the singlet state (S1) transfers to the triplet state (Tn, n ≥ 1) through the intersystem crossing (ISC) process, then the T1 excitons deactivate to the ground state (S0) via a phosphorescent radiation. The other is delayed fluorescence, in which the Tn transfers back to the S1 through reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) process, then the S1 excitons radiatively decay to S0 with a fluorescent radiation [5,6]. But the transition from S1 to Tn is a spin forbidden process. Meanwhile, the T1 easily releases energy in the form of non-radiative transitions via the motions of molecules/groups or the formation of interactions with the external environment, such as oxygen and solvents, which results in phosphorescence quenching [7–10]. So, the organic room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) is difficult to achieve. In order to achieve the RTP emission, heteroatom doping (n-π∗ transition) [11,12] and heavy atom effect (spin-orbit coupling effect) [13] are generally used to promote ISC process and generate a large number of triplet state excitons. Moreover, the organic materials usually have to form crystal structures [14–17], host-guest system [18–20], supramolecular assemblies [21,22], strong interactions [23–25] or encapsulation structures [26–28] to confine the motions of molecules/groups and isolate oxygen, resulting in stable triplet state. Based on the above strategies, many organic RTP materials have been fabricated. However, there are still some problems, such as complicated design or synthesis, short lifetime, limited colour and poor formability. Thus, the development of simple, universal and efficient strategies to construct new organic afterglow materials is a hot issue, and can significantly promote the richness and applications of organic optical materials.

The multi-colour afterglow materials can store the richer information, and are of significant importance to understand the photophysical process. However, most of the reported afterglow emissions are green or yellow-green. There are few studies on the other emission colors [29–35]. Wang et al. [29,30] embedded the carbon dots (CDs) into crystalline Mn-containing open-framework matrices or Mn2+ doped aluminophosphate zeolite frameworks to achieve a strong red phosphorescence. Wu et al. [34] embedded electron-rich organic phosphor into electron-deficient matrix polyacrylonitrile (PAN). Via tailoring noncovalent interactions between the PAN matrix and organic phosphors, a persistent red RTP composite was obtained. Dou et al. [31] introduced different luminophores onto sodium alginate chains to achieve color-tunable, excitation-dependent, and time-dependent phosphorescence. Sun et al. [33] doped brominated phenolsulfonephthaleine derivatives into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to achieve a near-infrared phosphorescence emission. How to achieve multi-colour afterglow luminescence are attracting more and more interest.

As the most abundant natural polymer on the earth, cellulose is considered as a promising and inexhaustible raw material which can meet sustainable development requirement of human society in future [36–38]. Thanks to the numerous hydroxyl groups on cellulose backbones, strong hydrogen bonds and crystallization in cellulose are formed, which is conducive to RTP. However, the natural cellulose exhibits weak phosphorescence intensity [39]. The further treatment of cellulose with NaOH was harmful to the phosphorescence [40]. Recently, phosphorescent cellulose derivatives were obtained by introducing ionic structures into cellulose chains [41,42]. However, it is a challenge to construct colorful phosphorescence materials from cellulose.

In this work, we used cellulose as the raw materials to fabricate N-doped cellulose-based CDs with phosphorescent properties in ionic liquid/DMF solution through a solvothermal method. Furthermore, a facile and universal strategy was demonstrated to achieve multi-colour afterglow emission, in which the cellulose-based CDs with phosphorescence emission were used as the energy donor, rhodamine derivatives with different fluorescence emission as the energy acceptor, and PVA with strong hydrogen bonding interactions as the matrix. Taking advantage of the electrostatic attraction interactions between the energy donor and energy acceptor, an efficient radiative energy transfer process was constructed to realize ultra-long multi-colour afterglow luminescence.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) with a degree of polymerization of 220 and 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl) were provided by Shandong Henglian New Materials Co., Ltd. Cellulose acetate (CA1.25, CA2.45, Mn = 30,000, DS = 1.25 and 2.45) was supplied by Nantong Cellulose Fibers Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), hydroxypropyl cellulos (HPC, Mn = 100,000), hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC, 28–30% methoxyl, 7–12% hydroxypropyl, viscosity: 6 mPa⋅s with 2% solution in 20 °C), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sulfo-cyanine3 carboxylic acid (Cy3) were purchased Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and methanol (MeOH) were chromatographic grade. Rhodamine 110 (Rh110), Rhodamine 123 (Rh123) and Rhodamine 6G (Rh6G) were purchased from J&K Scientific. Rhodamine B (RhB) was obtained from TCI Development Co., Ltd. Sulforhodamine 101 (Rh101) was obtained from Acros Organics. Fluorescein (Flu), 5-carboxyfluorescein (Flu-COOH), dichlorofluorescein (DCF), and Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and were purchased from Bide Pharmatech Ltd.

The cellulosed based N-doped CDs were synthesized by a solvothermal method as follows. The MCC (0.1296 g, 2 mmol) was dissolved in 9.072 g of AmimCl in a 25 mL two-necked flask with mechanically stirring at 80 °C for 2 h. Then, 3.888 g of DMF was added dropwise to obtain a homogeneous solution. The resultant MCC/AmimCl/DMF solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained suspension solution was purified through dialysis in a dialysis bag with MWCO of 1000 Da. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powders.

The 10 wt% PVA aqueous solution was obtained by dissolving 10 g of PVA in 90 g of H2O with stirring at 80 °C for 2 h. A certain concentration of CDs was added to the above 10 wt% PVA solution with stirring for 4 h. The resultant solution was used to prepare CDs-based film by natural volatilization of solvent. Finally, the film was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 6 h.

The rhodamine derivatives (Rhs) were dissolved in methanol. The Rhs solutions were blended with the CDs solution, then a certain amount of 0.1 M NaOH solution was added. After mixing, 4 g of 10 wt% PVA solution was added to the above solutions with stirring for 4 h to obtain the multi-colour afterglow inks, in which CDs was 2 wt% of the PVA matrix.

The rhodamine derivatives (Rhs) were dissolved in methanol. The Rhs solutions were blended with the CDs solution, then a certain amount of 0.1 M NaOH solution was added. After mixing, 3.2 g of 12.5 wt% CA2.45/DMF solution was added to the above solutions with stirring for 4 h to obtain the multi-colour fluorescence inks.
The Rhs/CDs/PVA solutions were used to prepare polymer films by natural volatilization of water. The films were dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 6 h.
The Rhs/CDs/PVA solutions were used to prepare fibers. The multi-colour afterglow fibers were prepared by a solution spinning process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out on a Hitachi HT-7700 with an accelerating voltage of 120 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi using 200 W monochromatic Al Kα radiation. Zeta potential was recorded on a Malvern Zetasize Nano-ZS90. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Time-resolved decay spectra were obtained on an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer equipped with a microsecond flash. Afterglow photographs were taken by a Sony α7 cameras. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (ΦPL) was measured by a FLS980 spectrometer with a calibrated integrating sphere in. In principle, the ΦPL can be expressed by the following Equation:
Φ
P L
=
ε α
=
n u m b e r
o f
e m i t t e d
p h o t o n s
n u m b e r
o f
a b s o r b e d
p h o t o n s
=
∫
300 750
L
e m i s s i o n
∫
E
r e f e r e n c e
− ∫
E
s a m p l e
where ε is the emitted photons of the sample that were collected by the integrating sphere in the range of 300–750 nm, and α is the photons absorbed by the sample. L
emission is the net luminescence of the sample. E
reference is the absorption spectrum of the reference in the sphere. E
sample is the absorption spectrum of the sample and reference.

In order to achieve multi-colour afterglow emission, we constructed an efficient energy transfer process between carbon dots (CDs) and common fluorescent molecules to transfer the phosphorescent energy to fluorescence. The cellulose-based phosphorescent CDs was used as the energy donor, and rhodamine derivatives (Rhs) were chosen as the energy receptors due to their adjustable fluorescence properties, high fluorescence quantum yields, good solubility, low cost and easy availability (Fig. 1 a). To achieve an efficient energy transfer, the distance between CDs and Rhs should be as close as possible, meanwhile the phosphorescent emission spectrum of CDs should overlap with the absorption spectra of Rhs as much as possible (Fig. 1b). The surface of CDs is positively charged, so the rhodamine derivatives were treated with NaOH to make them negatively charged. As a result, the CDs and Rhs can be close enough by the electrostatic attraction interactions, contributing to an efficient energy transfer process (Fig. S1). The CDs absorb the excitation light, and then the radiation oscillates leading to changes in the dipole distribution of CDs. In the absence of energy transfer, the excited CDs will return to the ground state (S0) with fluorescence or phosphorescence emission. The absorption spectra of Rhs strongly overlap with phosphorescent emission spectrum of CDs, while slightly overlap with fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1b). Therefore, the dipole oscillation of CDs can cause a change in the dipole distribution of Rhs. When the vibration frequencies of the two dipoles are the same, the energy transfer will occur between the CDs and Rhs. In addition, the triple state (T) energy of CDs is transferred to the singlet state (S) of Rhs through the T-S energy transfer process, then the singlet state excitons of Rhs return to the S0 in the form of fluorescence emission, realizing a red-shift afterglow emission (Fig. 1c). Based on this simple strategy, the colour of afterglow emission can be transformed from blue-green to green, yellow, orange and red. The emission wavelength shifts from 486 nm to 623 nm (Fig. 1e and 1f). Further, by changing the concentration ratio of rhodamine derivatives and CDs, the afterglow emission can be regulated, and the multi-colour afterglow emission with wide color gamut can be realized (Fig. 1f and Table S1).

As the most abundant natural polymer on the earth, cellulose is regarded as an inexhaustible raw material, and is one of the best eco-friendly sources for preparing CDs because of its polysaccharide structure and numerous active hydroxyl groups along the cellulose chain [37]. We used microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) as the carbon source, and 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl) and DMF as the solvent. After dissolving MCC, cellulose-based CDs were fabricated by a solvothermal process. The CDs solution emits blue fluorescence but no phosphorescence under ultraviolet light at room temperature. However, when the CDs solution was treated with liquid nitrogen or dispersed in PVA matrix, phosphorescence emission was observed because the motions of the surface groups of the CDs were inhibited (Fig. 2a and S2).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image demonstrates that the resultant cellulose-based CDs are well-dispersed nanoparticles with an average diameter of 4.62 nm (Fig. 2 b). When the excitation wavelength at 240–360 nm, the fluorescence performance of CDs has almost no excitation wavelength dependence, while the excitation wavelength at 360–450 nm, the fluorescence performance of CDs has an obvious excitation wavelength dependence (Fig. 2c). UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S3a) shows that CDs have a strong absorption peak at 212 nm and a broad absorption band at 250–400 nm. In the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs, there are two excitation bands with the centers at 265 nm and 340 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗ transition of C=C/C=N and the n-π∗ transition of C=O/C=N, respectively [43,44]. RTP spectrum of CDs has a broad excitation band with a maximum excitation wavelength at 280 nm, which is close to the fluorescence excitation peak. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) curves indicate that the CDs mainly consist of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and a small amount of Cl (Fig. S3b). The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows three peaks at 284.84 eV, 286.18 eV and 287.08 eV which are attributed to the C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N and C=O, respectively [45,46].

Cellulose is a linear polymer connected by cellobiose units with β-1,4 glycosidic bonds. There are numerous active hydroxyl groups along the cellulose chain. After a chemical modification of hydroxyl groups on cellulose, various cellulose derivatives with different hydroxyl contents can be obtained, such as cellulose acetate (CA1.25 and CA2.45), hydroxypropyl methyl cellulose (HPMC) and hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC). After mixing different cellulose derivatives with CDs, the phosphorescence properties have significant changes. CDs/HPMC and CDs/HPC show weak phosphorescence, while CDs/CA1.25 and CDs/CA2.45 have no phosphorescence (Fig. S4 and Table S3). These results indicate that the rigidity of polymer chains, the structure of derivative groups and the degree of substitution have a significant impact on the hydrogen bonding capabilities, which strongly affect the phosphorescent emission.

The photophysical process of phosphorescent emission of CDs is shown in Fig. 2e. Under UV light irradiation, CDs are excited from the ground state (S0) to the singlet excited state (Sn), which then relaxes to the first singlet state (S1) through internal conversion. The C=O and heteroatom N on the surface of CDs promote ISC process, contributing to the transition of excitons from S1 to T1. The triplet state excitons are very unstable and are prone to dissipate energy via non-radiative transitions. Therefore, room-temperature phosphorescence is difficult to be observed in CDs solution. Herein, PVA was introduced as the matrix due to its abundant hydroxyl groups, which can form strong hydrogen bonding interactions with surface groups (-OH, C=O, C–O/C–N, etc.) of CDs to stable the triplet state excitons [47,48]. In addition, the excellent oxygen barrier performance of PVA can avoid oxygen in the environment to quench the triplet sate excitons [49]. As a result, CDs/PVA solid phosphorescent material was achieved with optimal emission wavelength at 486 nm (λex = 270 nm) and lifetime of 916 ms (Fig. 2f and 2g, and Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy transfer process of multi-colour afterglow
ce:section.ce:para:
Take Rh123 for example, the fluorescence emission of Rh123/CDs/PVA under UV irradiation is a mixed spectrum of the blue fluorescence of CDs and the green fluorescence of Rh123. It gradually red-shifts as the Rh123 concentration increases (Fig. 3a and S5). When UV irradiation is turned off, Rh123/CDs/PVA exhibits afterglow emission, and the color of afterglow shifts from blue-green to green and yellow as the Rh123 concentration increases (Fig. 3a and S6). The detailed energy transfer process is as follows. The absorption spectrum of Rh123 overlaps strongly with the phosphorescence emission spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3 b), so the triplet state energy of CDs is easily transferred to the singlet state of Rh123 by a T-S energy transfer. Compared with CDs/PVA, Rh123/CDs/PVA has a new fluorescence emission at 500–600 nm, which is attributed to Rh123. With the increase of Rh123 content, the new fluorescence emission of Rh123/CDs/PVA gradually enhances, and the emission wavelength shifts from 532 nm to 551 nm (Fig. S5). Additionally, a new emission appears at 500–600 nm in the phosphorescence spectra of Rh123/CDs/PVA. With the increase of Rh123 content, the new afterglow emission intensity gradually increases, and the emission wavelength redshifts from 531 nm to 552 nm (Fig. 3g and S6), which is completely consistent with the fluorescence emission of Rh123 (Fig. S5). By contrast, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs in Rh6G/CDs/PVA decreases gradually (Fig. 3g and S6), and the afterglow lifetime decreases, as the Rh123 content increases (Fig. 3l and Table S4). The energy transfer efficiency is calculated according to the formula:
η =
(
1 −
τ
D A
τ D
)
× 100 %
where τDA and τD are the lifetime of the donor in the presence and absence of the acceptor, respectively [50]. When the Rh123 content is 0.25%, the lifetime decreases from 916 ms to 367 ms, and the energy conversion efficiency is 59.9% (Table S4).

Other rhodamine derivatives, such as Rh110, Rh6G, RhB and Rh101, exhibit similar phenomena. The absorption spectra of rhodamine derivatives overlap markedly with the phosphorescent emission spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3c–3f). Therefore, the triplet state energy of CDs is easily transferred to rhodamine derivatives. New emissions appear in the phosphorescence spectra of Rhs/CDs/PVA materials, and the intensity of the new afterglow emission enhances with the increase of Rhs content (Fig. 3h–3k and S7-S14). The new afterglow emission is consistent with the fluorescence emission wavelength of Rhs, and the emission wavelength red-shifts (Rh110/CDs/PVA, from 528 nm to 535 nm, Rh6G/CDs/PVA, from 560 nm to 582 nm, RhB/CDs/PVA, from 571 nm to 599 nm, Rh101/CDs/PVA, from 607 nm to 623 nm) with the increase of Rhs content (Figs. S7–S14). Moreover, the phosphorescence intensity of CDs and the afterglow lifetime decrease gradually, and the energy transfer efficiency increases, as the Rhs content increases (Fig. 3m–3p). For instance, when the Rh110, Rh6G, RhB and Rh101 content is 0.125%, the afterglow lifetime is 533 ms, 511 ms, 507 ms and 592 ms, and the conversion efficiency between CDs and Rhs is 41.8%, 44.3%, 44.7% and 35.4%, respectively (Tables S5–S8). Hence, the phosphorescent emission of the energy donor CDs is transferred to the receptor Rhs by means of T-S energy transfer, realizing the multi-colour afterglow.

The photoluminescence quantum yields (ΦPL) of Rhs/CDs/PVA are very high (up to 76.03%). The ΦPL of CDs aqueous solution is 20.7%, the ΦPL of CDs/PVA is 8.01%, and the phosphorescent quantum yield (ΦRTP) is 2.82% (Table S9). To further verify the universality of this strategy, commercial fluorescein derivatives such as fluorescein (Flu), 5-carboxyfluorescein (Flu-COOH), dichlorofluorescein (DCF), and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) and sulfo-cyanine 3 carboxylic acid (Cy3) were selected as energy acceptors to achieve multi-colour afterglow emission through T-S energy transfer process (Figs. S15–S19 and Tables S10–S14). The FL, RTP and lifetime properties of CDs/PVA were summarized according to be stored at different times, and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs almost remained constant under illumination of UV irradiation for 9000 s (Fig. S20 and Table S15). The above results indicate that the CDs have high photostability, and Rhs/CDs/PVA have high quantum yield and super-wide color gamut, suggesting that Rhs/CDs/PVA is suitable to application of stable advanced anti-counterfeiting and multiple-mode information encryption.

Multi-colour afterglow can not only avoid the interference from the scattered light and background fluorescence, but also provide more color changes and richer information, indicating extensive applications in solid state luminescence, information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, biological imaging and other fields. Based on the excellent formability and processability of the polymer matrix, we readily prepared Rhs/CDs/PVA films, coatings, fibers and patterns with both multi-colour fluorescence and afterglow (Fig. 4 ). Because PVA chains contain a large number of active hydroxyl groups, they can interact with various interfaces via hydrogen bonding interactions [51]. So, the Rhs/CDs/PVA materials are easy to adhere to the various substrates, such as metal, glass and plastic, to obtain multi-colour afterglow coatings (Fig. 4a). By a spinning process, the Rhs/CDs/PVA fibers with multi-colour afterglow can be prepared (Fig. 4b).

In addition, Rhs/CDs/PVA solutions can be utilized as inks to draw pictures on the ordinary printing papers. As shown in Fig. 4c, a flower picture consists of the flower (Rh6G/CDs/PVA ink: pink fluorescence and pink afterglow emission; RhB/CDs/PVA ink: pink fluorescence and orange afterglow emission; Rh101/CDs/PVA ink: pink fluorescence and red afterglow emission), stem (Rh110/CDs/PVA ink: green fluorescence and green afterglow emission) and leaves (CDs/PVA ink: blue fluorescence and blue-green afterglow emission). Under visible light, only the flower can be seen. Under UV light, fluorescence information appears. However, due to the strong blue background fluorescence of the ordinary printing paper, the fluorescence of CDs/PVA is hidden, and only the flower and the stem can be observed. After UV light is turned off, the background fluorescence of the printing paper disappears. The flower, stem and leaves can be observed. Moreover, the colorful patterns can give more information to achieve a high-level anti-counterfeiting. Based on the same strategy, the multi-colour afterglow materials can be used to encrypt information also. As shown in Fig. 4d, only “CAS” can be seen under visible light and UV light. When the UV light is turned off, all information “ICCAS 1956″ can be obtained.

Since Rhs/CDs/PVA reveals fluorescence and afterglow at room temperature while Rhs/CDs/CA has only fluorescence, more complex information encryption patterns can be designed by using them. As shown in Fig. 4e, a microarray data storage pattern has been constructed according to the binary codes of the standard 8-bit ASCII characters. The multi-colour and black colour represent ‘1’ and ‘0’, respectively. Under 365 nm UV lamp, each horizontal row shows the same fluorescence, called ‘wrong code’. The true information is hidden. Once the UV light was switched off, Rhs/CDs/PVA emits afterglow. The ‘real code’ can be obtained and translated into ‘1001001’ (blue-green), ‘1,000,011’ (green), ‘1,000,011’ (yellow), ‘1,000,001’ (orange) and ‘1,010,011’ (red), corresponding to the capital letters ‘I’, ‘C’, ‘C’, ‘A’ and ‘S’ according to ASCII binary code. Furthermore, the encryption and transmission of information can be also achieved by using Chinese traditional telegraph code (Fig. 4f). Under UV irradiation, the multi-colour patterns of ‘8888’ and ‘8888’ exhibit. When the UV light was turned off, the patterns change into ‘8955’ and ‘7820’, which can be decoded as the Chinese characters ‘tan’ and ‘dian’.

Natural polymer cellulose rich in C and O was utilized as carbon source to prepare cellulose-based N-doped phosphorescent CDs by a solvothermal method. The resultant CDs were then dispersed in PVA matrix to obtain solid RTP material. Subsequently, the common rhodamine derivatives with different fluorescent emission were added to absorb the phosphorescence energy of CDs. Via an efficient T-S energy transfer process, multi-colour afterglow emission from green to yellow, orange and red was achieved. The resultant Rhs/CDs/PVA materials with tunable afterglow were readily processed into films, fibers, coatings and patterns with colorful fluorescence and afterglow, claiming a huge potential in advanced anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, etc.

Kunfeng Jin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xin Ji: Methodology, Data curation. Jinming Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Qinyong Mi: Methodology. Jin Wu: Methodology. Jun Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U2004211 and 52173292), the National Key Research and Development Project (No. 2020YFC1910303), and the Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (No. 2018040).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article: UV–Vis absorption spectra; Fluorescence excitation spectra; RTP excitation spectra; XPS spectra; Three-dimensional fluorescent plots; Three-dimensional RTP plots; Time-resolved decay spectra; Photographs; Zeta potential; CIE coordinates.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2022.101179.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-lived emissive probes for time-resolved photoluminescence bioimaging and biosensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.Y. Zhang, Q. Yu, H. Wei, S. Liu, Q. Zhao, W. Huang, Long-Lived Emissive Probes for Time-Resolved Photoluminescence Bioimaging and Biosensing, Chem Rev 118 (2018) 1770-1839. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.chemrev.7b00425.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing the performance of pure organic room-temperature phosphorescent luminophores

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kenry, C. Chen, B. Liu, Enhancing the performance of pure organic room-temperature phosphorescent luminophores, Nat Commun 10 (2019) 2111. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-019-10033-2.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Förster resonance energy transfer: an efficient way to develop stimulus-responsive room-temperature phosphorescence materials and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, J. Yang, M. Fang, Y. Yu, B. Zou, L. Wang, Y. Tian, J. Cheng, B.Z. Tang, Z. Li, Forster Resonance Energy Transfer: An Efficient Way to Develop Stimulus-Responsive Room-Temperature Phosphorescence Materials and Their Applications, Matter 3 (2020) 449-463. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matt.2020.05.005.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in persistent luminescence based on molecular hybrid materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Gao, M.S. Kodaimati, D. Yan, Recent advances in persistent luminescence based on molecular hybrid materials, Chem Soc Rev 50 (2021) 5564-5589. https://doi.org/10.1039/d0cs01463j.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Afterglow of carbon dots: mechanism, strategy and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, Y. Wang, Z. Li, H. Lin, Afterglow of carbon dots: mechanism, strategy and applications, Materials Chemistry Frontiers 4 (2020) 386-399. https://doi.org/10.1039/c9qm00578a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theory of long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence in organic aggregates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Peng, H. Ma, Z. Shuai, Theory of Long-Lived Room-Temperature Phosphorescence in Organic Aggregates, Acc Chem Res 54 (2021) 940-949. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.accounts.0c00556.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triplet—triplet annihilation and delayed fluorescence in molecular aggregates

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Sternlicht, G.C. Nieman, G.W. Robinson, Triplet-Triplet Annihilation and Delayed Fluorescence in Molecular Aggregates, The Journal of Chemical Physics 38 (1963) 1326-1335. https://doi.org/10.1063/1.1733853.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.M. Schulman, C. Walling, Phosphorescence of adsorbed ionic organic molecules at room temperature, Science 178 (1972) 53-54. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.178.4056.53.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room temperature phosphorescence of organic compounds. The effects of moisture, oxygen, and the nature of the support-phosphor interaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.M. Schulman, R.T. Parker, Room temperature phosphorescence of organic compounds. The effects of moisture, oxygen, and the nature of the support-phosphor interaction, The Journal of Physical Chemistry 81 (2002) 1932-1939. https://doi.org/10.1021/j100535a010.
